era_context: Late Bronze Age, transition from Amenhotep III to Amenhotep IV (Akhenaten) in Egypt. The Hittite Empire under Šuppiluliuma I is at its peak. Mycenaean Greece is in Late Helladic IIIA. The Shang Dynasty continues in China. This is a pivotal moment in Egyptian history, marking the beginning of the Amarna Period.

events.title: Amenhotep IV (Akhenaten) assumes power; beginning of Amarna Period
events.description: Amenhotep IV, later known as Akhenaten, assumes sole or primary power following the death of Amenhotep III (conventionally dated ~1352 BCE, though the exact date is debated). He begins a radical religious and political revolution, promoting the worship of the Aten (sun disk) and eventually establishing a new capital at Akhetaten (modern-day Amarna). This marks the beginning of the Amarna Period, one of the most transformative eras in Egyptian history. The shift from traditional polytheism to Atenism is revolutionary and will have profound consequences for Egyptian religion, art, and politics.

sources.name: Temple inscriptions, reliefs, and administrative records from Amarna
Later Egyptian king lists and chronicles

events.certainty_note: Akhenaten's reign is well-attested, but the exact date of his accession is debated. Conventional dating places it around 1353 BCE, with some scholars proposing dates as late as 1340 BCE or as early as 1365 BCE. The Amarna Letters provide contemporary documentation of his reign.

events.title: Šuppiluliuma I's continued dominance; Hittite-Egyptian tensions
events.description: Šuppiluliuma I continues to dominate the Hittite Empire and maintain control over Syria and the Levant. The transition in Egypt to Akhenaten's rule may have been perceived as a moment of weakness, and Hittite pressure on Egyptian-controlled territories continues. Diplomatic correspondence (the Amarna Letters) reflects ongoing tensions between the Hittite and Egyptian powers over control of the Levant.
events.certainty_note: Šuppiluliuma's dominance is well-documented. The Amarna Letters provide contemporary evidence of Hittite-Egyptian tensions.

events.title: Mycenaean Greece in Late Helladic IIIA; palace cultures flourishing
events.description: Mycenaean palace cultures in Greece are flourishing during Late Helladic IIIA. Major palatial centers at Mycenae, Tiryns, Pylos, and Thebes are at their height. Trade networks connect the Aegean to Egypt, the Levant, and Anatolia. Linear B script is in use for administrative purposes. The Mycenaean civilization is at its peak, though the causes of the later collapse (around 1200 BCE) are not yet evident.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from palace sites (Mycenae, Tiryns, Pylos)

events.certainty_note: The Late Helladic IIIA period is well-attested archaeologically. Specific events within individual years are not documented in contemporary sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Akhenaten's accession is debated. Some scholars place it as early as 1365 BCE; others as late as 1340 BCE. The conventional date of ~1353 BCE is based on astronomical calculations and the Amarna Letters, but these are not definitive. Some Egyptologists question the extent of Akhenaten's religious revolution, arguing that Atenism was not as radical a break from traditional religion as once thought. The relationship between Akhenaten's reign and the Hittite expansion is not explicitly documented; the connection is inferred from the Amarna Letters and the political context.
historiographic_note: Sources for 1336 BCE are richer than for earlier years in this batch, thanks to the Amarna Letters and the abundant archaeological evidence from Amarna itself. However, the Amarna Letters are diplomatic correspondence, not a comprehensive historical record, and they reflect Egyptian perspectives. Hittite sources are official royal inscriptions, which are also biased. The dating of events in this period relies on astronomical calculations, king lists, and synchronisms with other chronologies, all of which have margins of error. Mycenaean Greece is documented archaeologically but not through contemporary written sources (Linear B is administrative, not historical narrative). Most of the world remains undocumented.

graph_edges.note: Akhenaten's reign continues and deepens the religious revolution
Continuation of Hittite dominance and Hittite-Egyptian tensions
Akhenaten's rise coincides with Šuppiluliuma I's dominance; the Amarna Letters document their interaction
